stormy.label: Storm watch
stormy.note: Great cafe day. Pick somewhere with backup seating and stay close to metro.

rainy.label: Rainy Istanbul
rainy.verb: The city is in ferry-window mode.
rainy.note: Kadikoy, Karakoy, and Galata still work. Just choose shorter walks and covered tables.

windy.label: Bosphorus wind
windy.note: Good day for a shorter crossing, then a stable indoor work block.

cloudy.label: Soft gray city
cloudy.verb: Long walks, low glare, good focus.
cloudy.note: A strong day for neighborhood scouting before the evening tables fill up.

sunny.label: Bright Bosphorus
sunny.note: Walk the water, take the ferry, then cool down at a laptop-friendly cafe.

kadikoy.name: Kadikoy
kadikoy.oneLiner: Walkable Asian-side hub with ferry access and some of the city's best independent cafes.

moda.name: Moda
moda.oneLiner: Calm seaside peninsula with creative energy, long evening walks, and a lot of cats.

cihangir.name: Cihangir
cihangir.oneLiner: Bohemian hilltop with Bosphorus views, strong cafe culture, and a tight expat community.

besiktas.name: Besiktas
besiktas.oneLiner: Lively waterfront district with a big market, student energy, and ferry access on both sides.

galata.name: Karakoy / Galata
galata.oneLiner: Design district between the bridge and the tower. Specialty coffee, galleries, and central access.

uskudar.name: Uskudar
uskudar.oneLiner: Traditional Bosphorus base with ferry links, quiet streets, and sunset walks by Maiden's Tower.

nisantasi.name: Nisantasi
nisantasi.oneLiner: Polished, central, and cafe-rich. Good for nomads who want comfort, boutiques, gyms, and easy access to Sisli.

levent.name: Levent
levent.oneLiner: Business-district base with metro access, malls, offices, and the most practical coworking density.

balat.name: Balat
balat.oneLiner: Colorful Golden Horn neighborhood with character, cheaper rents, hills, and slower cafe days.

atasehir.name: Atasehir
atasehir.oneLiner: Modern Asian-side high-rise district for corporate work, newer apartments, malls, and quieter routines.

neighborhoods.question: Which neighborhood should I start in?
neighborhoods.answer: Kadikoy on the Asian side is the most popular with nomads - walkable, affordable, and packed with cafes. Cihangir on the European side is the go-to for a social, bohemian vibe with Bosphorus views.

cost-of-living.question: How much does a month in Istanbul cost?
cost-of-living.answer: A comfortable nomad lifestyle runs $1,200-$1,500/month including a furnished apartment in Kadikoy, eating out a few times a week, coworking, and transport. Budget-conscious nomads can manage on $750-$1,000.

visa.question: Do I need a visa to work remotely?
visa.answer: For stays under 90 days, a tourist e-visa ($50-60, instant online) is all you need. For longer stays, Turkey has a digital nomad visa (12 months, requires $3,000/month income) or a standard residence permit.

internet.question: How do I get a SIM card?
internet.answer: Buy a Turkcell tourist SIM at any company-owned store for about 250 TL (~$8) with 20 GB data. Bring your passport. City stores are cheaper than airport counters.

coworking.question: Where can I work with good wifi?
coworking.answer: Kolektif House has 100+ Mbps wifi and costs 490 TL/month (~$16). For cafes, Kadikoy and Cihangir have the highest density of laptop-friendly spots with reliable connections.

housing.question: How do I find an apartment?
housing.answer: Start with Airbnb or Flatio for your first month while you explore. For longer stays, Sahibinden.com has the best local prices. Always visit in person before paying.

safety.question: Is Istanbul safe?
safety.answer: Yes. Istanbul is very safe for its size. The nomad-popular neighborhoods (Kadikoy, Cihangir, Besiktas, Karakoy) are all safe day and night. Use BiTaksi for taxis and standard big-city precautions.

transport.question: How do I get from the airport?
transport.answer: The Havaist bus is the best value (150 TL, runs to Taksim and Kadikoy). A taxi costs 600-800 TL. The new M11 metro line also connects the airport to the city.

culture.question: Do I need to speak Turkish?
culture.answer: Not in nomad-friendly areas - English is widely understood in cafes, coworking spaces, and tourist areas. But learning 20 basic phrases will transform how locals treat you.

healthcare.question: How do I see a doctor?
healthcare.answer: Private hospitals (Acibadem, Memorial) have English-speaking staff and accept international insurance. A doctor visit costs 500-1,500 TL (~$16-48) without insurance - much cheaper than Western countries.

meta.title: About
meta.description: The story behind Istanbul Digital Nomads - how we started, what we believe in, and the people who make it happen.

story.title: Our Story
story.p1: Istanbul Digital Nomads is a brand-new community, started in 2026 by one person who decided to make the move and wanted to build the thing that didn't exist yet for the next people coming.
story.p2: The decision came in February 2026. The idea took shape in March. The website went live in April. The first meetup is planned for Q2. That's the whole story so far - we're writing the rest with whoever shows up.
story.p3: Istanbul is one of the best cities in the world for remote work - affordable, culturally rich, extremely well-connected, and full of energy. Our mission is to make the transition easier and the experience richer for every nomad who comes here.

values.title: What We Believe In
values.description: The principles that guide our community.

inclusive.title: Inclusive
inclusive.description: Everyone is welcome regardless of background, skill level, or how long they plan to stay.

helpful.title: Helpful
helpful.description: We share knowledge freely - from visa tips to the best cafes. No question is too basic.

authentic.title: Authentic
authentic.description: Real connections over networking. We value genuine relationships and honest recommendations.

fun.title: Fun
fun.description: Life as a nomad should be enjoyable. We balance productivity with exploration and social events.
